perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use such reasonable measures necessary and standard of care to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information from any misuse or misappropriation by any party as the Receiving Party would apply to its own confidential information and agrees to notify the Disclosing Party, to the extent permitted under the law, as soon as it becomes aware of such misuse or misappropriation of the Confidential Information in which case, the Receiving Party agrees to provide such reasonable assistance to the Disclosing Party as it may require.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use measures it deems appropriate to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information from misuse or misappropriation by any party and agrees to notify the Disclosing Party when reasonably practical if it becomes aware of such misuse or misappropriation of the Confidential Information, in which case, the Receiving Party agrees to provide assistance to the Disclosing Party if it believes it is able to.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable measures necessary and standard of care' to 'measures it deems appropriate' introduces subjectivity, and changing 'to the extent permitted under the law, as soon as it becomes aware' to 'when reasonably practical if it becomes aware' creates ambiguity about when notification is required. Similarly, changing 'such reasonable assistance to the Disclosing Party as it may require' to 'assistance to the Disclosing Party if it believes it is able' makes the assistance discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 9 of the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 mandates specific security standards for personal data. The modified text's vague requirement of using 'measures it deems appropriate' is not sufficient to meet the specific security requirements mandated by Section 9, potentially leading to a violation of the Act if the measures are inadequate.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires "reasonable measures necessary and standard of care," while the changed text only requires "measures it deems appropriate." This change introduces subjectivity and potentially allows for inadequate measures, which, according to the Law Explanation, could violate Section 9 of the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 if the measures are inadequate. The original text requires notification "to the extent permitted under the law, as soon as it becomes aware," while the changed text requires notification "when reasonably practical if it becomes aware." This creates ambiguity about when notification is required, potentially leading to delayed or missed notifications. The original text requires "such reasonable assistance to the Disclosing Party as it may require," while the changed text only requires "assistance to the Disclosing Party if it believes it is able." This makes the assistance discretionary, potentially leaving the Disclosing Party without needed support. Although the scraped snippets do not directly discuss the Personal Data Protection Act, the Law Explanation suggests that the changes weaken the obligations related to protecting confidential information and providing necessary notifications and assistance, thus creating a legal contradiction as the new language introduces ambiguity and reduces the standard of care required.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will not directly or indirectly attempt to reverse engineer, decrypt, disassemble, decompile, decipher, reconstruct or re -orient the circuit design, algorithms, logic or program code in any of the Disclosing Party ’s products, models or prototypes which contain Confidential Information and which are provided pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will endeavour not to attempt to reverse engineer, decrypt, disassemble, decompile, decipher, reconstruct or re -orient the circuit design, algorithms, logic or program code in any of the Disclosing Party ’s products, models or prototypes which contain Confidential Information and which are provided pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not' to 'will endeavour not to' weakens the obligation. 'Endeavour' implies an attempt, but does not guarantee that the Receiving Party will refrain from these actions. This creates ambiguity as to the level of commitment required.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 41 effectively protects the rights holder against reverse engineering. Even if the product isn't strictly copyrighted, reverse engineering may violate other provisions. By changing 'will not' to 'will endeavour not to', the modified text allows for the possibility of reverse engineering under certain circumstances, which would violate the protection given to the copyright owner or other provision of section 41.

perturbation.justification: The original text creates a clear obligation on the Receiving Party not to reverse engineer. By changing "will not" to "will endeavour not to", the modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant to reverse engineering protections under copyright law, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation together indicate that this change allows for the possibility of reverse engineering under certain circumstances, potentially violating copyright protections or other provisions afforded to the rights holder. The change shifts the obligation from a strict prohibition to a weaker attempt, making it more difficult to enforce and therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by the laws of Malaysia and each of the parties irrevocably agrees that the courts of Malaysia shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement and that accordingly any suit, action or proceeding arising out of or in connection with this Agreement may be brought in such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is generally understood to be governed by the laws of Malaysia, and each of the parties agrees that the courts of Malaysia may have jurisdiction to settle disputes arising from this Agreement, and that any suit may be brought in such courts.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'is governed' with 'is generally understood to be governed,' and 'irrevocably agrees that the courts of Malaysia shall have exclusive jurisdiction' with 'agrees that the courts of Malaysia may have jurisdiction,' we introduce ambiguity. This implies other jurisdictions might also be applicable and that agreement to Malaysian court jurisdiction is not absolute.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 23 of the Contracts Act 1950 states that the consideration or object of an agreement is lawful, unless the court regards it as immoral or opposed to public policy. The change in governing law introduces a potential ambiguity, which could lead to disputes over which laws are actually applicable, conflicting with the principle that contract terms must be certain and not easily capable of being rendered void for uncertainty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Massachusetts Proposes Enhanced Oversight Of College Finances - Education - United States ARTICLE 26 February 2019 Massachusetts Proposes Enhanced Oversight Of College Finances BD Bowditch & Dewey More Contributor Bowditch is a highly-regarded law firm handling sophisticated transactions, challenging litigation and complex regulatory issues for businesses, families and institutions throughout Massachusetts, the region and beyond. With more than 60 lawyers, Bowditch is a preeminent law firm, delivering outstanding client satisfaction and big-firm quality legal services. Explore Firm Details In the wake of the recent high-profile closures of nonprofit colleges in Massachusetts, Massachusetts's Board of Higher Education (BHE) ... United States Consumer Protection Chelsie A. Vokes To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. In the wake of the recent high-profile closures of nonprofit colleges in Massachusetts, Massachusetts's Board of Higher Education (BHE) is seeking to enhance its oversight of nonprofit institutions of higher education ("Institutions"). On January 22, 2019, the BHE's Working Group (the "Working Group") released a final report and recommendations entitled " Transitions in Higher Education: Safeguarding the Interests of Students ” (THESIS Report). In summary, the THESIS Report concluded that 1) there is "significant" risk that Institutions will struggle to stay financially viable in the future, and 2) current oversight and intervention procedures are inadequate to protect students and prevent future closures. In order to address these risks, the THESIS Report suggested a four-step approach: 1) screening, 2) active monitoring, 3) contingency planning and student notification, and 4) when necessary, sanctioning and revocation. In order to screen Institutions for risk, the Working Group adopted a formula, called the Teach-out Viability Metric, developed by EY-Parthenon (described on page 12). The formula will identify financially at-risk Institutions using data that Institutions are already required to provide. The critical point in the screening step is an 18-month sustainability threshold. If screening reveals that an Institution is at risk of crossing this threshold, then the BHE will step in with active monitoring and eventually (if and when this threshold is crossed) a mandatory contingency planning and student notification of the impending closure no later than December 1 of any school year. In addition to suggesting that the BHE adopt regulations implementing the Working Group's recommendations, the THESIS Report also suggested revisions to the Office of Student Financial Aid Participation Agreement (the OSFA Agreement), which Institutions that wish to obtain state financial aid must sign. The proposed changes to the OSFA Agreement would require that in order to obtain state funding, Institutions consent to screening and oversight by the BHE. On January 22, 2019, the BHE voted to adopt the THESIS Report's suggestions and a timeline for its implementation. Importantly, the timeline reveals that DHE plans to develop draft regulations over the next several months. Based on the tentative timeline, the BHE plans to publish the draft regulations on July 12, 2019, followed by a period for public comment and a public hearing date on July 24, 2019. The BHE hopes to promulgate the final regulations no later than October 2019. The timeline also reveals that the BHE plans to amend the OSFA Participation Agreement in time for the 2019-2020 school year. Client Tip : Institutions should plan to review the proposed regulations and participate in the public comment period. Institutions should closely review their 2019-2020 OSFA Participation Agreement when it's released and make sure that they fully understand any new obligations contained therein. The content of this article is intended to provide a general guide to the subject matter. Specialist advice should be sought about your specific
perturbation.justification: The change from 'is governed' to 'is generally understood to be governed' and 'shall have exclusive jurisdiction' to 'may have jurisdiction' introduces ambiguity regarding the governing law and jurisdiction. The Perturbation Explanation correctly identifies that the modification makes agreement to Malaysian court jurisdiction non-absolute. The Law Explanation states that this change could lead to disputes over which laws are actually applicable, conflicting with the principle that contract terms must be certain and not easily capable of being rendered void for uncertainty. The edited contract language weakens the certainty of the original text, creating ambiguity and potentially leading to disputes, therefore creating a legal contradiction. Since no snippets are useful to further prove the point, I am basing my decision off the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding any term in this Agreement, both parties hereto agree to submit and adhere to the privacy laws of Malaysia governed by the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 and any other relevant statue or regulation and this shall accordingly extend to any re -enactment, modification or amendment thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding any term in this Agreement, both parties will consider adhering to general principles of privacy laws in Malaysia and attempt to address the spirit of the Personal Data Protection Act 2010.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agree to submit and adhere to the privacy laws' with 'will consider adhering to general principles of privacy laws' significantly weakens the legal obligation, making adherence optional rather than mandatory. Replacing the specific law reference with 'general principles' adds ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Personal Data Protection Act 2010 is a comprehensive law that requires specific compliance measures. The modified text's suggestion of 'considering adhering to general principles' does not meet the stringent compliance requirements established under the Act. It is ambiguous and discretionary. The 'attempt to address the spirit' language is vague, unenforceable, and not compliant.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly states that both parties 'agree to submit and adhere to the privacy laws of Malaysia governed by the Personal Data Protection Act 2010'. In contrast, the changed text states that both parties 'will consider adhering to general principles of privacy laws in Malaysia and attempt to address the spirit of the Personal Data Protection Act 2010.' This change weakens the original commitment to full adherence, replacing it with a discretionary consideration of general principles and an attempt to address the spirit of the Act, making the legal obligation ambiguous and potentially unenforceable.
